title: Dilated Cardiomyopathies
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium
authors: Neal K. Lakdawala, Lynne Warner Stevenson, Joseph Loscalzo

items.title: Understanding the Progression and Recovery Potential in Dilated Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) often progresses into a convergent clinical phenotype similar to acute myocardial infarction. Secondary responses, including neurohormonal modulation and cardiac resynchronization therapy, can be modifiable or reversible. Almost a third of patients with recent-onset DCM without coronary artery disease show substantial spontaneous recovery to normal ejection fraction.
keyPoints: DCM progresses into a phenotype similar to acute myocardial infarction.
Secondary responses are often modifiable or reversible.
Almost a third of patients with recent-onset DCM demonstrate substantial spontaneous recovery to normal ejection fraction.

items.title: Role of Alcohol in Alcoholic Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Alcohol is the most common toxin implicated in chronic DCM. Alcoholic cardiomyopathy causes more hospital admissions in men than women, but prevalence is similar between genders with alcoholism. Left ventricular dysfunction is detected in about a third of asymptomatic patients. The estimated alcohol intake necessary to cause cardiomyopathy is 3–4 ounces or ≥60–80 g of pure ethanol daily for ≥5 years.
keyPoints: Alcohol is the most common toxin implicated in chronic DCM.
Hospital admissions due to alcoholic cardiomyopathy are more frequent in men.
Left ventricular dysfunction detected in about a third of asymptomatic patients with alcoholism.
Estimated alcohol intake necessary for cardiomyopathy: 3–4 ounces or ≥60–80 g of pure ethanol daily for ≥5 years.

items.title: Genetic and Environmental Factors in DCM
items.content: Multiple causes contribute to DCM, including myocarditis, genetic variants, toxic and metabolic disorders. Polymorphisms of genes encoding alcohol dehydrogenase and the angiotensin-converting enzyme may influence the likelihood of alcoholic cardiomyopathy. Mutations in TTN and other DCM disease genes are found in ~10% of patients with presumed alcohol cardiomyopathy.
keyPoints: Multiple causes contribute to DCM, including myocarditis and genetic variants.
Polymorphisms in genes encoding alcohol dehydrogenase and angiotensin-converting enzyme may influence alcoholic cardiomyopathy risk.
Mutations in TTN and other DCM disease genes found in ~10% of patients with presumed alcohol cardiomyopathy.

items.title: Reversibility of Alcoholic Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Alcoholic cardiomyopathy can have a reversible component, with marked improvement possible within 3–6 months of abstinence. However, continued heavy alcohol consumption leads to a grim prognosis.
keyPoints: Cardiac impairment includes both permanent damage and reversible components.
Improvement can occur in 3–6 months with abstinence.
Prognosis worsens with ongoing heavy alcohol use.

items.title: Anthracycline-Induced Cardiotoxicity
items.content: Anthracyclines like doxorubicin cause cardiotoxicity through mechanisms such as reactive oxygen species and mitochondrial damage. Risk increases with factors like older age, obesity, hypertension, and higher doses.
keyPoints: Histologic changes include vacuolar degeneration and myofibrillar loss.
Risk factors: older age, obesity, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, preexisting cardiac disease.
Systolic dysfunction may not result in marked left ventricular dilation.

items.title: Trastuzumab-Related Cardiotoxicity
items.content: Trastuzumab can cause cardiotoxicity, especially when combined with anthracyclines. While often reversible, it may persist in about a third of patients and progress to heart failure.
keyPoints: Cardiotoxicity is higher with concurrent anthracycline use.
Less toxicity observed with current combination regimens compared to past.
About one-third of affected patients experience persistent cardiomyopathy.

items.title: Cardiotoxicity with VEGF Inhibitors
items.content: VEGF inhibitors, used in cancer therapy, can cause cardiotoxic effects due to their impact on cardiovascular signaling pathways. These include hypertension and proteinuria similar to preeclampsia, which are risk factors for future cardiac disease.
keyPoints: VEGF inhibitors affect cardiovascular systems by altering vasodilator-vasoconstrictor balance and glomerular function.
Cardiotoxicity may present with peripheral fluid accumulation rather than elevated central venous pressures.
Management includes treating hypertension, withdrawing the inhibitor if possible, and conventional heart failure treatment.

items.title: Proteasome Inhibitors and Cardiovascular Risks
items.content: Proteasome inhibitors like carfilzomib, used for multiple myeloma treatment, are associated with increased risks of hypertension, ischemic events, thromboembolism, and heart failure.
keyPoints: Carfilzomib is more cardiotoxic than bortezomib.
Immunomodulatory drugs like lenalidomide and thalidomide also pose cardiovascular risks including heart failure and venous thromboembolism.

items.title: Cobalt Toxicity and Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Chronic exposure to cobalt can lead to cardiomyopathy, often presenting with polycythemia, hypothyroidism, and goiter. The condition is linked to impaired myocardial energetics.
keyPoints: Cobalt toxicity may result from industrial exposures or cobalt alloy prosthetic hips.
Diagnosis involves confirming cobalt exposure and identifying intramitochondrial particles via electron microscopy.

items.title: Understanding Risk Factors for PPCM
items.content: Peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) is influenced by several risk factors, including older maternal age, increased parity, twin pregnancy, malnutrition, and tocolytic therapy. Hypertensive disorders during pregnancy, such as preeclampsia, are also significant contributors.
keyPoints: 1:1000 to 1:4000 deliveries in the U.S. affected by PPCM
Risk fourfold higher in black women
Up to half of cases occur with hypertensive disorders like preeclampsia

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Initial Management of PPCM
items.content: PPCM typically presents with congestion, defined by an LVEF ≤ 0.45 near the end of pregnancy without another cardiac diagnosis. Diagnosis is often confirmed via echocardiography, while MRI can be considered in complex cases.
keyPoints: LVEF ≤ 0.45 for PPCM diagnosis
Echocardiography usually sufficient; avoid gadolinium in MRI
Initial treatment includes loop diuretics and collaboration with maternal-fetal medicine team

items.title: Prognosis and Recovery Factors for PPCM
items.content: Recovery of LVEF to ≥ 0.50 occurs in 50–80% of PPCM cases, often within six months. Recovery is less likely with initial LVEF < 0.30, larger left ventricular end-diastolic dimension, black race, and presentation >6 weeks post-delivery.
keyPoints: LVEF improvement to ≥ 0.50 in 50–80% of cases
Less recovery likelihood with LVEF < 0.30 or LV dimension ≥ 0.60
One-year mortality rates range from 4% to 11%, higher in black women

items.title: Thyroid Disorders and Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Endocrine disorders like hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism can exacerbate heart failure, though they rarely cause it in an otherwise normal heart. Hyperthyroidism is a key consideration in cases of new-onset atrial fibrillation or difficult-to-control rapid ventricular response. Hypothyroidism requires careful management to avoid worsening tachyarrhythmias and heart failure.
keyPoints: Thyroid function tests are routine for evaluating cardiomyopathy.
Hyperthyroidism is linked with new-onset atrial fibrillation or difficult-to-control rapid ventricular response.
Hypothyroidism should be treated slowly to avoid exacerbating tachyarrhythmias and heart failure.
Close supervision during antithyroid medication titration is crucial due to risk of precipitous decompensation.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.title: Pheochromocytoma and Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Pheochromocytoma, though rare, should be considered in patients with heart failure, labile blood pressure, and episodic palpitations. It often presents with postural hypotension and requires surgical removal after initial treatment with alpha-adrenergic receptor antagonists.
keyPoints: Consider pheochromocytoma in heart failure with labile BP and heart rate.
Patients may exhibit postural hypotension.
Treatment involves alpha-adrenergic receptor antagonists followed by surgical extirpation.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.title: Obesity and Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Obesity is associated with cardiomyopathy, often overlapping with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. It contributes to impaired excretion of excess volume load, leading to increased wall stress and secondary adaptive neurohumoral responses. Weight reduction can significantly improve cardiac function.
keyPoints: Obesity can lead to cardiomyopathy, often overlapping with HFpEF.
Impaired volume excretion and increased wall stress are key factors.
Effective weight reduction is associated with improved ejection fraction and clinical function.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Hemochromatosis-Related Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Hemochromatosis, a metabolic condition leading to iron overload, can manifest as dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM). Diagnosis is based on serum iron and transferrin saturation levels (>60% for men, >45–50% for women), with MRI used to quantify iron stores. Early diagnosis allows management through phlebotomy or chelation therapy if necessary.
keyPoints: Hemochromatosis can present as DCM.
Diagnosis: serum iron and transferrin saturation >60% (men), >45–50% (women).
MRI quantifies cardiac iron stores; biopsy not usually needed.
Management includes phlebotomy or chelation therapy with desferrioxamine or deferasirox.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Characteristics and Management of Takotsubo Syndrome
items.content: Takotsubo syndrome, or stress-induced cardiomyopathy, typically affects older women after intense stress. It presents with apical ballooning on imaging and mimics acute myocardial infarction but lacks coronary artery occlusion. Management includes nitrates for pulmonary edema, alpha/beta blockers if stable, magnesium for arrhythmias, and occasionally an intra-aortic balloon pump.
keyPoints: Occurs in older women after intense stress.
Features apical ballooning; mimics acute myocardial infarction.
Management: nitrates, alpha/beta blockers, magnesium, possible intra-aortic balloon pump.
Prognosis is generally good with low mortality.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Takotsubo Syndrome and Coronary Embolus
items.content: Takotsubo syndrome can be differentiated from coronary embolus by the absence of atherosclerotic disease. Both present similarly but require different diagnostic approaches, such as coronary angiography to rule out acute coronary occlusion in takotsubo.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis includes coronary embolus without atherosclerosis.
Coronary angiography is needed to exclude acute coronary occlusion.
Recurrence rate of takotsubo syndrome is 10%, with an annual recurrence rate of 2%.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.question: Which of the following is a common secondary development as dilated cardiomyopathy progresses?
items.explanation: Mitral regurgitation commonly develops as the ventricle dilates and the valvular apparatus is distorted.

items.question: What percentage of patients with recent-onset cardiomyopathy in the absence of coronary artery disease demonstrate substantial spontaneous recovery to normal ejection fraction?
items.explanation: Almost a third of patients with recent-onset cardiomyopathy in the absence of coronary artery disease demonstrate substantial spontaneous recovery to normal ejection fraction.

items.question: What is often required as part of recommended therapy for chronic dilated cardiomyopathy?
items.explanation: Recommended therapy includes neurohormonal modulation, cardiac resynchronization therapy for left bundle branch block, and diuretics as needed.

items.question: What is a common cause of cardiotoxicity leading to chronic dilated cardiomyopathy?
items.explanation: Alcohol is the most common toxin implicated in chronic DCM.

items.question: Which of the following is a common cardiac complication in patients with severe alcoholic cardiomyopathy?
items.explanation: Atrial fibrillation occurs commonly both early in the disease ('holiday heart') and in advanced stages.

items.question: What is a characteristic histologic change caused by anthracyclines like doxorubicin?
items.explanation: Anthracyclines cause characteristic histologic changes of vacuolar degeneration and myofibrillar loss.

items.question: Which therapy is used to prevent anthracycline cardiomyopathy but has concerns about reducing cancer treatment efficacy?
items.explanation: The use of dexrazoxane, an intracellular iron chelating agent, can prevent anthracycline cardiomyopathy.

items.question: What is a common cardiac complication associated with regular cannabis use?
items.explanation: Regular cannabis use has been linked to increased risk of atrial fibrillation.

items.question: Which class of drugs used for lymphoid malignancies is associated with cardiovascular risks such as atrial and ventricular arrhythmias?
options: Tyrosine kinase inhibitors, specifically Bruton tyrosine kinase inhibitors
items.explanation: Bruton tyrosine kinase inhibitors (e.g., ibrutinib) are associated with cardiovascular risks including atrial and ventricular arrhythmias.

items.question: Which agent is noted to be more cardiotoxic than bortezomib in the treatment of multiple myeloma?
items.explanation: Carfilzomib is more cardiotoxic than bortezomib in the treatment of multiple myeloma.

items.question: What is a common cardiovascular effect observed with tyrosine kinase inhibitors?
options: Peripheral fluid accumulation due to elevated central venous pressures
Hypertension and proteinuria similar to preeclampsia
Decreased ejection fraction with restrictive phenotype
items.explanation: Tyrosine kinase inhibitors can cause peripheral fluid accumulation due to local factors rather than elevated central venous pressures.

items.question: Which class of drugs used for systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis may decrease ejection fraction?
items.explanation: Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine can decrease ejection fraction with either restrictive or dilated phenotype.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a risk factor for peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM)?
items.explanation: High levels, not low levels, of soluble FLT1 are associated with worse outcomes in PPCM.

items.question: Which population has a fourfold higher risk of developing PPCM?
items.explanation: Risk of PPCM is fourfold higher in black women.

items.question: What is the usual time frame for PPCM to present after delivery?
items.explanation: Most cases of PPCM present within the first week after delivery.

items.question: Which medication is contraindicated during pregnancy due to adverse fetal effects?
items.explanation: Renin-angiotensin system inhibitors should not be given during pregnancy due to adverse fetal effects.

items.question: Which condition should be considered when a patient presents with new-onset atrial fibrillation or ventricular tachycardia that is difficult to control?
items.explanation: Hyperthyroidism should be considered with new-onset atrial fibrillation or ventricular tachycardia that is difficult to control.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.question: What is the definitive therapy for pheochromocytoma?
items.explanation: Definitive therapy for pheochromocytoma requires surgical extirpation.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.question: What can cause cardiomyopathy due to obesity?
options: Impaired excretion of excess volume load
items.explanation: Obesity is associated with impaired excretion of excess volume load, which can lead to cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.question: Which deficiency can cause severe chronic heart failure that responds to calcium repletion?
items.explanation: Chronic deficiencies of calcium can cause severe chronic heart failure, which responds to calcium repletion.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: Which diagnostic method is principally used to diagnose cardiac iron overload in hemochromatosis?
options: Serum iron and transferrin saturation measurement
MRI quantitation of liver and heart iron stores
items.explanation: MRI is used to quantify iron stores in the liver and heart, making it principal for diagnosing cardiac iron overload.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: What is the typical demographic most affected by takotsubo syndrome?
options: Older women after sudden intense emotional or physical stress
items.explanation: Takotsubo syndrome typically occurs in older women after sudden intense emotional or physical stress.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: What is a common feature of the ventricular shape in takotsubo syndrome?
options: Global ventricular dilation with apical ballooning
items.explanation: Takotsubo syndrome is characterized by global ventricular dilation with apical ballooning.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended management strategy for takotsubo syndrome?
options: Selective beta blockade if hemodynamically stable
Magnesium for arrhythmias related to QT prolongation
Intra-aortic balloon pump in absence of left ventricular outflow tract obstruction
items.explanation: Combined alpha and beta blockers are recommended rather than selective beta blockade if hemodynamically stable.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.statement: Genetically predicated alcohol consumption has been linked to heart failure.
items.explanation: Mendelian randomization studies have not identified a link between genetically predicated alcohol consumption and heart failure.

items.statement: Alcoholic cardiomyopathy is more prevalent in men than women.
items.explanation: Prevalence of alcoholic cardiomyopathy is similar between men and women with alcoholism.

items.statement: Cocaine can produce chronic cardiomyopathy and acute ischemia.
items.explanation: Cocaine, amphetamines, and related catecholaminergic stimulants can produce chronic cardiomyopathy as well as acute ischemia.
Although more often reversible than anthracycline cardiotoxicity, trastuzumab cardiomyopathy may persist in about a third of affected patients.

items.statement: Cobalt cardiomyopathy is often associated with polycythemia, hypothyroidism, and goiter.
items.explanation: Cobalt cardiomyopathy typically presents with these symptoms due to cobalt's effects on red cell production and thyroxine.

items.statement: The primary mechanism of toxicity for chloroquine in causing cardiomyopathy is impaired lysosomal function.
items.explanation: Chloroquine's presumed mechanism of cardiac toxicity involves impaired lysosomal function, leading to accumulation of inclusion bodies.

items.statement: PPCM can present with an LVEF greater than 0.45.
items.explanation: Criteria for PPCM include an LVEF ≤ 0.45.

items.statement: Anticoagulation is usually prescribed for the first 6 weeks after delivery in patients with PPCM and LVEF <0.35.
items.explanation: PPCM with LVEF <0.35 carries increased risk of left ventricular thrombus, so anticoagulation is typically prescribed.

items.statement: Diabetes mellitus is a typical factor in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.
items.explanation: Diabetes mellitus is typically associated with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.statement: Nutritional deficiencies are commonly implicated in causing dilated cardiomyopathy in developed countries.
items.explanation: Nutritional deficiencies can occasionally cause DCM but are not commonly implicated in developed countries.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.statement: Endomyocardial biopsy is usually required to diagnose cardiac iron overload in hemochromatosis.
items.explanation: Diagnosis of cardiac iron overload is principally made by MRI, and biopsy is not usually needed.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.statement: The long-term prognosis for takotsubo syndrome is generally poor.
items.explanation: The long-term prognosis for takotsubo syndrome is generally good.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.assertion: Alcoholic cardiomyopathy causes many more hospital admissions in men than women.
items.reason: Prevalence is similar between men and women with alcoholism, but left ventricular dysfunction is detected in about a third of asymptomatic patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the reason does not explain why there are more hospital admissions.

items.assertion: Mutations in TTN can be identified in a significant percentage of patients with presumed alcohol cardiomyopathy.
items.reason: ~10% of patients with presumed alcohol cardiomyopathy have mutations in DCM disease genes like TTN.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Patients with severe alcoholic cardiomyopathy can experience marked improvement within 3–6 months of abstinence.
items.reason: The cardiac impairment in severe alcoholic cardiomyopathy includes a substantial component that is reversible after cessation of alcohol consumption.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Anthracycline cardiotoxicity generally results in marked left ventricular dilation.
items.reason: Systolic dysfunction can occur acutely with symptoms of heart failure noted soon after drug administration.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because anthracycline cardiotoxicity does not result in marked left ventricular dilation, while the reason is true.

items.assertion: Combined immune checkpoint inhibitors can cause fatal inflammation affecting the heart.
items.reason: These drugs block T-cell suppression, leading to potentially fatal inflammation directed at multiple organs including the heart.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Tyrosine kinase inhibitors always cause elevated central venous pressures.
items.reason: These drugs can lead to peripheral fluid accumulation due to local factors rather than elevated central venous pressures.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because tyrosine kinase inhibitors do not always cause elevated central venous pressures.

items.assertion: PPCM recovery is less likely in black women.
items.reason: Recovery of normal LVEF takes longer and is less likely in black women compared to white women.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Genetic testing is not necessary for PPCM patients.
items.reason: Positive genetic testing predicts less recovery in the mother and can impact family members.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; genetic testing should be considered as it has implications for both the mother and family.

items.assertion: Effective weight reduction is often associated with major improvement in ejection fraction and clinical function in obese patients.
items.reason: Obesity alone can lead to impaired excretion of excess volume load, which over time increases wall stress and secondary adaptive neurohumoral responses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.assertion: Pheochromocytoma is commonly associated with postural hypotension.
items.reason: Patients with pheochromocytoma often have labile blood pressure and heart rate, sometimes with episodic palpitations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why pheochromocytoma is associated with postural hypotension.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 1)

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.assertion: Hemochromatosis can be managed by repeated phlebotomy if diagnosed early.
items.reason: Phlebotomy removes excess iron from the body, reducing cardiac and other complications.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.assertion: Takotsubo syndrome is caused by atherosclerotic coronary artery disease.
items.reason: The condition can be triggered by intense emotional or physical stress leading to myocardial dysfunction.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as takotsubo syndrome occurs in the absence of significant coronary artery disease.
items.reference: Dilated Cardiomyopathies · METABOLIC CAUSES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY (part 2) | TAKOTSUBO SYNDROME OR CARDIOMYOPATHY
